title: Team Adoption Rollout
plainEnglishSummary: Introduce AI without fear — pilots, company rules, better prompting, and stopping tool chaos.

reading.script:
If you're going to roll AI out to your team, this week is about doing it without creating fear or chaos.

Start with the conversation. Frame AI as an assistant, not a replacement. We're removing boring first drafts, not your judgment. Acknowledge the fear honestly — then show one workflow where AI saves thirty minutes on something they already hate doing.

Rollout plan. Pick one to three pilot use cases with volunteers — not a company-wide mandate on day one. Write simple AI rules: approved tools, no pasting client secrets, humans send external messages. Train prompting with two or three copy-paste templates. Document workflows in one shared doc. Prevent random tool overload — one primary chat tool, add others only with approval.

That's how adoption actually sticks. Alright — activity when you're ready.

sections.heading: The conversation to have
paragraphs: Frame AI as an assistant, not a replacement: "We're removing boring first drafts, not your judgment."
Acknowledge fear honestly — then show one workflow where AI saves thirty minutes they hate.

sections.heading: Rollout plan
paragraphs: Pick one to three pilot use cases with volunteers — not company-wide mandate on day one.
Write simple AI rules: approved tools, no pasting client secrets, human sends external messages.
Train prompting with two or three copy-paste templates. Document workflows in one shared doc.
Prevent random tool overload — one primary chat tool, add others only with approval.

discussPrompts: What's your first pilot use case for the team?
What 2 rules would go on your company AI one-pager?

steps.situation: Team hears 'we're adopting AI' and fears layoffs.
steps.question: Best leadership message?
options: AI handles first drafts of tasks you hate — your calls stay yours
AI will replace half the team by Friday
steps.feedback: Assistant framing + concrete time-save example reduces fear.

steps.question: Best approach?
options: 1–3 voluntary pilots with clear before/after time tracking
Let everyone experiment with no rules
steps.feedback: Small pilots with metrics beat big-bang chaos.

steps.situation: Sales rep wants to paste full client contracts into a random free AI site.
steps.question: Company rule?
options: Approved tools only; redact sensitive data; no unvetted apps
steps.feedback: Approved stack + redaction = minimum viable policy.

steps.situation: Support team keeps writing weak prompts and getting generic replies.
steps.question: Best fix?
options: Shared prompt templates + 30-min training with examples
Buy a more expensive model only

steps.situation: Three departments each bought different AI subscriptions.
steps.question: Prevent overload by…
options: Standardize on 1 primary chat tool; approve exceptions
Add a fourth tool for balance
steps.feedback: One primary tool simplifies training, security, and cost.

steps.situation: Pilot succeeded — weekly meeting notes → action email saves 40 min.
steps.question: Next step?
options: Document the workflow and train one more team
steps.feedback: Document + expand beats jumping to full automation.

takeaway: Assistant not replacement. Pilots, rules, templates, one primary tool — then scale.
